characteristics: The CTA is the most important actionable element, tied to the primary goal (sales/signups). It must capture immediate attention via visual weight.
dos: Use a unique color or style that doesn't compete with other screen elements. Ensure enough contrast with the background. Use wording that calls to an action.
donts: Do not use more than one instance of a primary CTA on one page to avoid dividing user attention. Do not use ghost buttons for main CTAs.

characteristics: Visual hierarchy directs the user to what matters most. De-emphasizing secondary information makes the interface less noisy.
dos: Use font weight and color instead of just size to control hierarchy; de-emphasize tertiary content using light grey.
donts: Don't let every element compete for attention; don't use font weights under 400 for small UI text.

characteristics: Use of the design is easy to understand, regardless of the user's experience, knowledge, language skills, or current concentration level.
dos: Eliminate unnecessary complexity; be consistent with user expectations and intuition.
donts: Not in source

characteristics: Users have a pre-existing representation in their heads of how a product or system works based on past experience.
dos: Do research to understand the mental models of your target audience. Design the conceptual model to match the mental model.
donts: Don't let the conceptual model 'bubble up' from the technology/database structure.

characteristics: The design is useful and marketable to people with diverse abilities.
dos: Provide the same means of use for all users: identical whenever possible; equivalent when not.
donts: Avoid segregating or stigmatizing any users.

characteristics: Users use visual design (look and feel) as the first indicator of whether to trust a site.
dos: Use professional layout, color, and font to pass the initial 'trust rejection' phase. Ensure content is credible for long-term trust.
donts: Don't neglect first impressions; users will reject a site based on poor design before even reading the content.

characteristics: Design modules in the master Sketch file must have an exact equivalent in the code.
dos: Ensure names in the Sketch file and the code match exactly.
donts: Let art direction and engineering get out of sync.

characteristics: Research-driven design to ensure the right product is built for the user.
dos: Conduct research, analyze data, and talk to users before making design assumptions.
donts: Make assumptions about user requirements without grounded data.

characteristics: The highest priority is to satisfy the customer through early and continuous delivery of valuable software.
dos: Release updates and new versions frequently to meet changing needs. Focus on high-value activities.
donts: Wait for long release cycles (e.g., 18-24 months) or until the end of a cycle to deliver functionality.

characteristics: The team reflects on how to become more effective, then tunes and adjusts behavior.
dos: Conduct regular retrospectives to identify and learn from what is or isn't working.
donts: Ignore team feedback or shut down conversation during review sessions.

characteristics: Familiarity with shapes associated with action improves recognition speed. Deviation increases identification time.
dos: Use rectangles or rounded rectangles as the safest choice. Ensure buttons stand out from other elements.
donts: Do not use organic or irregular shapes (triangle, circle) unless the style requires it.

characteristics: Physical size affects the ease of tapping or clicking. Too small targets lead to frustration and errors.
dos: Start with 44 x 44 points for mobile (50 points ideal). For cursor-based devices, 32 x 32 points is acceptable.
donts: Do not make buttons smaller than the recommended minimum.

characteristics: Contrast ratio determines readability and accessibility for users, including those with impaired vision.
dos: Aim for at least AA contrast ratio between labels and backgrounds. Use saturation that works in light/dark modes.
donts: Do not use low contrast ratios that make buttons look like decoration; avoid high contrast that causes eye strain.

characteristics: Clarity of the primary message or action takes precedence over aesthetics.
dos: Identify the single most important thing you want users to see and do first.
donts: Fragment the user experience with conflicting messages or multiple equal-weight calls to action.

characteristics: Designing for exploration and error prevention.
dos: Design interactions so they are impossible to mis-tap; treat UI like a children's toy.
donts: Create fragile interfaces where users can easily make irreversible errors.

characteristics: Prioritizing long-term utility and accessibility over temporary design trends.
dos: Adopt technology only when it serves a proven purpose for the majority of users.
donts: Introduce stylistic features like parallax effects just for the sake of following a trend.

characteristics: Every element must have a purpose contributing to the larger goal.
dos: Remove any element if its purpose cannot be explicitly explained.
donts: Add decorative elements that do not contribute to functionality or core brand message.

characteristics: Distinguishing between navigation and data manipulation.
dos: Use buttons for changing/manipulating data; use links for navigating between pages.
donts: Use primary button styles for simple navigation tasks that don't involve state changes.

characteristics: Working software is the primary measure of progress.
dos: Evaluate progress by the incorporation of design into functional code; demonstrate user stories.
donts: Judge progress solely by the number of design artifacts produced or documentation.

characteristics: Continuous attention to technical excellence and good design enhances agility.
dos: Continually refine design decisions throughout the cycle.
donts: Treat design as a one-time activity at the kickoff or milestone.

characteristics: The art of maximizing the amount of work not done is essential. Focus on essential user needs.
dos: Focus only on what is most necessary; seek the simplest way to write code.
donts: Spend time on wireframes for implemented designs; squeeze in features without evaluating impact.

characteristics: The brain can only process a small amount of information at a time. Providing only what is needed avoids overwhelming users.
dos: Show people what they need when they need it. Reveal details only as needed contextually.
donts: Don't give too much information all at once. Don't worry about counting clicks if they reduce cognitive load.

characteristics: Different demands use different mental resources. Cognitive load is the most expensive.
dos: Minimize cognitive load even if it increases motor load (clicks). Ensure targets are large enough (Fitt's Law).
donts: Don't make people think or remember more than necessary. Don't make targets too small or far apart.

characteristics: Time is relative; mental processing and expectations influence frustration.
dos: Always provide progress indicators (skeleton screens, average-color blocks). Keep task time consistent.
donts: Don't make users wait without feedback; don't use empty white screens or center-page spinners only.

characteristics: Providing instant responses to user actions even while background validations occur to reduce perceived latency.
dos: Register an action instantly in the UI while syncing in the background.
donts: Do not show a loading spinner for every minor interaction; don't make users wait for complex checks.

characteristics: Motion fits naturally into the experience with intention to help users reach goals.
dos: Use animation to surface connections between states or provide feedback.
donts: Do not use motion just for the sake of moving; avoid 'jokey' indicators for critical services.

characteristics: Optimal timing changes based on viewport size and element complexity.
dos: Use 100-200ms for small components; 500-700ms for page transitions.
donts: Don't make users 'watch the clock' by using slow progress indicators or spinners.

principle: Start with a feature, not a layout
characteristics: The shell of the app is secondary to functionality. Designing features first provides navigation context.
dos: Start with a piece of actual functionality, like a search field or a specific button.
donts: Don't start by designing the navigation bar or the page container.

characteristics: Abstractly figuring out every edge case is inefficient. It is easier to solve problems in a usable interface.
dos: Work in short cycles: design a simple version, build it, and iterate on the working design.
donts: Don't try to design every single feature and every edge case before moving to implementation.

characteristics: Having too many options causes decision fatigue. Constraints facilitate consistency.
dos: Define a restrictive type scale and a limited color palette (8-10 shades) in advance.
donts: Don't pick values like font sizes or hex codes one pixel or shade at a time from a limitless pool.

principle: Text needs consistent contrast on images
characteristics: Background images are dynamic and can make text unreadable.
dos: Add a semi-transparent overlay; lower image contrast; or add a soft text shadow to increase legibility.
donts: Don't place text directly on a busy image without a contrast-improving treatment.

characteristics: Design decisions should be based on target audience demographics and physical context.
dos: Account for viewing distance, indoor/outdoor use, and input methods; adjust text size and contrast.
donts: Don't ignore the suitability of the interface style for age groups; don't apply mobile variables to TV.

characteristics: Inclusive design benefiting all users, including those with impairments.
dos: Use minimum 12pt font (16pt preferred); build accessibility into the process from the start.
donts: Don't rely on one sense alone; don't use serif type or italics for large blocks; avoid 'Click here'.

characteristics: Maintaining predictable behavior and layout across multiple screens.
dos: Keep margins and placement of common items (title bars, back buttons) the same; use grids.
donts: Don't allow objects to 'jump around' between different views of the interface.

characteristics: Sizing interactive elements for finger/thumb operation.
dos: Use a minimum touch area of 7mm x 7mm; leave at least a 2mm gap between items.
donts: Don't place touch targets too close together, leading to accidental presses.

characteristics: Designing to minimize user mistakes through constraints, feedback, and safe arrangement.
dos: Use warnings and confirmations; provide default values; allow undos.
donts: Don't let users cruise through hazardous procedures on 'autopilot'.

characteristics: Making the overall experience feel like a coherent service across multiple devices.
dos: Specify which functionality belongs on each device; create guidelines that span multiple device types.
donts: Don't treat devices as isolated cells; avoid disjointed UIs.

characteristics: Interfaces depend on visual representation of objects and rapid, reversible operations.
dos: Ensure interface actions are aligned with user understanding; provide immediate feedback.
donts: Don't distance the user from the consequences of their actions.

characteristics: Allowing users to experience benefits before requiring investment of effort or data.
dos: Let users engage with core features before asking them to sign up; prompt for sign-in during task saving.
donts: Don't use 'Sign up' and 'Sign in' together as the terms are confusingly similar.

characteristics: Agile processes harness change for competitive advantage, even late in development.
dos: Solicit feedback often; embrace additions or subtractions from the prioritized list.
donts: Stick blindly to a roadmap; lock down requirements very early (Waterfall style).

characteristics: Deliver working software frequently, with a preference for shorter timescales.
dos: Aim for sprints typically between one and four weeks; deliver shippable functionality.
donts: Allow prototypes to remain unavailable; rely on long-release cycles that cause feature lag.

characteristics: Build projects around motivated individuals; give them support and trust.
dos: Trust the team to make necessary decisions that affect the final output.
donts: Treat staff as interchangeable resources rather than human beings with unique skills.

characteristics: Design communicates necessary info regardless of ambient conditions or sensory abilities.
dos: Use different modes (pictorial, verbal, tactile) for redundant presentation of essential information.
donts: Not in source

characteristics: The design accommodates a wide range of individual preferences and abilities.
dos: Provide choice in methods of use; accommodate right- or left-handed access.
donts: Not in source

principle: Feature Broad Range of Types on Homepage
characteristics: First-time users rely on homepage visuals to infer the site's product catalog and breadth. Narrow selections lead to misinterpretation.
dos: Ensure a broad selection of different product types are featured; use images or thumbnails; dedicate space to non-popular categories.
donts: Only feature a small selection of product types; assume users will know the site sells other items.

principle: Filters vs. Categories for Shared Attributes
characteristics: Wrongly implementing filters as categories prevents users from mixing criteria (e.g., 'mummy' + 'down' sleeping bag).
dos: Use categories for mutually exclusive hierarchy scopes; use filters for attributes where multiple can be selected.
donts: Implement product type as a category if attributes are the same across types; use brand-based categories.

characteristics: Redundancy makes it difficult to know which option to select and makes the site look unprofessional.
dos: Manually create the taxonomy; map manufacturer data to your own curated categories; check for combined categories.
donts: Use auto-generated categorization; reuse manufacturer categories without merging across vendors.

characteristics: Users struggle to find and verify compatibility (e.g. laptop chargers). Completion rates are very low without explicit cross-linking.
dos: Create unique pages for each compatibility product; include series-level pages; link these in cross-sell sections.
donts: Assume users know their model's specific specs; force users to manually compare dimensions from different pages.

principle: Avoid Relying Solely on Thematic Categories
characteristics: Users expect a site to be structured by 'type of product.' Purely thematic navigation breaks mental models.
dos: Use thematic paths as secondary options; ensure the core navigation is based on product types.
donts: Replace product type categories with brand or occasion categories entirely.

principle: Main Nav Categories as First Hierarchy Level
characteristics: Hiding categories behind a singular "Products" link causes double-hover issues and prevents inferring site type at a glance.
dos: Place top-level categories permanently in the main navigation bar; use non-product categories (e.g. "Deals") alongside them.
donts: Scope the entire catalog within a solitary "Products" item; use double-hover activation areas with short heights.

characteristics: Non-product links (Contact, Sign-in, Shipping) clutter the header but are vital for those who need them.
dos: Use white space or background colors to separate courtesy links; group them into sections; include a 'Shipping' link.
donts: Place courtesy navigation below primary product navigation; omit vital policy links from the header entirely.

principle: Have First 1-2 Levels as Sub-Category Pages
characteristics: Sub-category pages act as storefronts, clearing up ambiguity via thumbnails and descriptions before showing products.
dos: Display sub-categories high on the page; include thumbnails; provide a "View All" option for each scope.
donts: Display a product list for broad categories; push sub-category links below the fold with featured products.

principle: Avoid Dividing or Sub-Dividing Product Lists
characteristics: Thematic divisions make lists hard to sort and can lead users to think they've reached the end of results prematurely.
dos: Use product type filters instead of sectional dividers; show a single continuous list of products.
donts: Divide lists into thematic sections on one page; use product sliders or tabs to sub-divide featured results.

characteristics: To minimize deviations, modules are specified precisely in master files.
dos: Define typography treatments, use an 8-pixel grid for spacing, and document interactions precisely.
donts: Allow inconsistent naming or deviations from the master Sketch file across platforms.
